title: W3 D Suggested Exercises
body:
Suggested Exercises for Week 3
                                      Juan Carlos Carbajal

                                ECON 3202 — Term 1, 2026
                                          UNSW Sydney


Problem 1. Consider the following real-valued functions. For each case, identify whether the
function is weakly increasing, strictly increasing, or strictly increasing for strict differences.
 (a) Let f ( x1 , x2 ) = c1 x1 + c2 x2 defined on R2 for c1 , c2 > 0.
 (b) Let g( x1 , x2 ) = ln x1 + ln x2 defined on R2++ .
  (c) Let d0 : R2 → R + be the Euclidean distance to 0.
 (d) Let dz : R n → R + be the Euclidean distance to z.
  (e) Let u( x1 , x2 ) = x1α1 x2α2 defined on R2+ for α1 , α2 > 0.
  (f) Let v( x1 , x2 ) = min{ x1 , x2 } defined on R2++ .


Problem 2. Consider the following two real-valued functions defined on R:
                                              󰀻
                                              󰀿0     for x ≤ 0,
                                    f (x) =
                                             󰀽1      for x > 0,
                                             󰀻
                                             󰀿0      for x rational,
                                    g( x ) =
                                             󰀽1      for x irrational.

 (a) Show that f is continuous everywhere except at x = 0.
 (b) Show that g is discontinuous everywhere.


Problem 3. Let x, y be any two real numbers.
 (a) Show that x2 − y2 = ( x − y)( x + y).
 (b) Show that x3 − y3 = ( x − y)( x2 + xy + y2 ).
  (c) Show that x4 − y4 = ( x − y)( x3 + x2 y + xy2 + y3 ).

  We can extend the argument to any n ∈ N, and write
                               󰀃                                                          󰀄
          x n − y n = ( x − y ) x n −1 + x n −2 y + x n −3 y 2 + . . . + x y n −2 + y n −1 .   (1)

This identity is worth remember, but you don’t have to show it.



                                                     1
 Problem 4. Using the 󰂃, δ definition of continuity, show that the following functions are con-
tinuous on R.
  (a) f ( x ) = x;
  (b) g( x ) = x2 ;
  (c) h( x ) = x3 ;
 (d) ψ( x ) = x n , for all n ∈ N.

Hint: One strategy for this question is to use the identity in Equation 1 to show part (d), and the rest
will follow immediately by letting n be equal to 1,2, and 3, respectively. But try to solve parts (a) to (c)
first, to get intuition for (d). The key observation is that if x and y are sufficiently close to each other,
then the summation that appears in the right-hand side of Equation 1 is bounded.


Problem 5. Let f : R → R be defined as
                                                  󰀻
                                                  󰀿x      if x ∈ Q,
                                        f (x) =
                                                  󰀽0      if x ∕∈ Q.

Prove that f is continuous at x̂ = 0. Is f continuous at any other point in R?


Problem 6. In class we mentioned that any constant function is continuous. In this problem,
you are asked to show this formally for functions defined on R and on R n .
  (a) Let f : R → R. Show that if f is constant, then it is continuous.
  (b) Let g : R n → R. Show that if g is constant, then it is continuous.


Problem 7. There is only one composite good in this economy, which can be consumed in positive
or negative amounts (think of a perfectly divisible numeraire and allow consumers to take
negative positions). Anne has preferences represented by a continuous utility function u : R →
R.
   In class we saw that if Anne strictly prefers to consume x to y, then consuming slightly
more, or less, than x should also be strictly preferred than consuming y. This result can be
strengthen to the following proposition, which you are asked to prove.

   Proposition 1

   If Anne’s utility function u : R → R is continuous and u( x ) > u(y), then there exists
   some δ > 0 such that for all x ′ ∈ ( x − δ, x + δ) and all y′ ∈ (y − δ, y + δ), one has
   u ( x ′ ) > u ( y ′ ).


   In words, the continuity of Anne’s utility function implies that if she strictly prefers x to
y, then any x ′ that is sufficiently close to x will also be strictly preferred to any y′ that is
sufficiently close to y. Anne’s preferences do not exhibit sudden reverses.




                                                     2
